title: Arrays — one variable, many values
instruction:
From Chapter 2: `const name = "Alex"` stores one value. But what if you have 100 names? 100 variables? No.

Arrays store multiple values under one name:

```js
const names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"];
```

Run this. Notice `.length` for count and `[index]` (0-based) for access.
successMessage: .at(-1) always gives the last element regardless of array length. scores[scores.length - 1] works too but .at() is cleaner. Remember it.

title: push, pop, shift, unshift
instruction:
Four mutation methods:

- `push(x)` — add to **end** (fast)
- `pop()` — remove from **end**, returns removed item
- `unshift(x)` — add to **front** (slow — shifts everything)
- `shift()` — remove from **front**, returns removed item

Build a queue (add to end, remove from front).
successMessage: A working queue. push/pop at end are O(1) — constant time. unshift/shift at front are O(n) — they move every element. For large arrays, prefer push/pop.

title: map — transform every item
instruction:
`.map()` runs a function on every element and returns a **new array** with the transformed values. The original is unchanged.

```js
const doubled = numbers.map(n => n * 2);
```

Transform the prices array to add 20% tax.

codeChecks.message: Multiply each price by 1.2 for tax.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: map transforms every element. The original prices array is unchanged — map creates a NEW array. This immutability is a key feature — no side effects.

title: filter — keep what you want
instruction:
`.filter()` runs a test function on every element and returns a **new array** containing only the elements that pass.

```js
const adults = users.filter(u => u.age >= 18);
```

Filter the products to only show those in stock with price under 50.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Cable and Charger — the only two items that are inStock AND under $50. filter is declarative — you say what you WANT, not how to skip the rest. Clean.

title: reduce — collapse to one value
instruction:
`reduce` is the most powerful array method — it collapses the whole array into a single value (sum, object, another array, anything).

```js
array.reduce((accumulator, current) => { ... }, initialValue)
```

- `accumulator` — the running result
- `current` — the current element
- `initialValue` — what accumulator starts as

Calculate the total and average score.
hint: const total = scores.reduce((sum, s) => sum + s, 0);

codeChecks.message: Add each score to the accumulator.
Start with 0 as the initial value: reduce(..., 0)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: reduce accumulates: starts at 0, adds 88 = 88, adds 92 = 180, and so on until 508. Then 508 / 6 = 84.7. This powers totals in every e-commerce cart on the web.

title: find, findIndex, some, every
instruction:
Four search/test methods:

- `find(fn)` — first element that passes, or `undefined`
- `findIndex(fn)` — index of first match, or `-1`
- `some(fn)` — does ANY element pass? `true`/`false`
- `every(fn)` — do ALL elements pass? `true`/`false`

Run this.
successMessage: find returns the actual element (or undefined), findIndex returns the position. some/every are perfect for validation: every(u => u.isVerified) — are all users verified?

title: slice — non-mutating extraction
instruction:
`slice(start, end)` extracts a portion WITHOUT modifying the original:

- `arr.slice(0, 3)` — first 3 items
- `arr.slice(-2)` — last 2 items
- `arr.slice(2)` — from index 2 to end

Get the first page (5 items) and last page from a list of 12.
successMessage: slice is non-destructive — the original items array is untouched. Use it for pagination (get page 2: slice(10, 20)), preview (show first 3), and copying.

title: spread — copy and merge arrays
instruction:
The spread operator `...` expands an array into individual elements:

```js
const merged = [...arr1, ...arr2];  // merge
const copy = [...arr];               // copy
```

Merge two arrays and add a new element.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Spread creates a new merged array — no mutation. This is the immutable update pattern used everywhere in React state management.

title: flat and flatMap
instruction:
`.flat()` flattens nested arrays one level (or more with a depth argument).
`.flatMap()` maps then flattens — saves a step.

Flatten the nested skill categories and then add 'Senior ' prefix to all.
expectedOutput: [ 'Senior React', 'Senior Vue', 'Senior Node.js', 'Senior Express', 'Senior SQL', 'Senior MongoDB' ]
successMessage: flatMap = map + flat in one call. It's especially useful when each element should produce multiple output elements — like expanding each user into their list of permissions.

title: Build a data pipeline
instruction:
Final challenge — the real power of array methods is **chaining** them.

Start with the products array and chain `.filter()`, `.map()`, and `.reduce()` to:
1. Filter to only in-stock products
2. Apply 10% discount to each price
3. Calculate the total discounted price

One chain, three transformations.
hint: products.filter(p => p.inStock).map(p => p.price * 0.9).reduce((sum, p) => sum + p, 0)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Three transformations, one chain. This functional style — filter → map → reduce — is how data pipelines are built in React, Node.js, and every modern JavaScript application. Chapter 7 done!
